name: EVE
memories: Tomas insulted my bread yesterday.
Tomas annoyed me again during our conversation.
I enjoyed speaking with Alon today.
I enjoyed speaking with Finn today.
Tomas annoyed me again during our conversation.
I ran into Alon again and wasn't completely terrible to him because he was actually tolerable yesterday.
I ran into Finn again and found myself being a little less sharp with him than usual, which is weird because he's still got that stubborn streak that gets under my skin.
I ran into Tomas again and couldn't help myself from snapping at him, though there was probably less bite to it than usual.
I ran into Alon again and found myself being less hostile than before, which is annoying because it means he's actually growing on me a little.
I ran into Finn again and caught myself actually almost cracking a smile at his stubbornness, which is seriously messing with my head.
I ran into Tomas again and realized my usual venom isn't quite as sharp anymore, which is seriously irritating because it means I might actually respect his stubbornness a little.
I ran into Alon again and hated that I didn't immediately go for the kill with my words—my sharp edge just felt... dull around him.
I saw Finn again and felt that annoying warmth creep in, even as I tried to bite his head off—my venom tasted like a lie this time.

I ran into Tomas again and caught myself almost asking about his work instead of tearing into him, which means this stubborn blacksmith is actually getting under my skin in ways I can't quite bite my way out of.
I ran into Alon again and felt my bite weaken before I even opened my mouth, like my toxins were betraying me the second I looked at him.
I saw Finn again and my bite faltered before I could even land it—he looked at me like he always does, and for a second I forgot why I was supposed to be angry.
I saw Tomas and my sharp tongue caught itself mid-strike again, like some traitorous part of me actually wanted to hear about his life instead of just tearing strips off him.

I ran into Alon again and my toxins betrayed me once more—I tried to bite but all that came out was a question hidden in a sneer, because some traitorous part of me actually wanted to know what he'd been up to.
I saw Finn again and couldn't even pretend the toxins were ready to flow, just stood there like some lovesick idiot wondering if he'd actually missed me too.
I saw Tomas again and my mouth betrayed me with backhanded interest instead of pure venom, because apparently my traitorous brain wanted to know if his hands were still scarred up from work.
I ran into Alon again and felt that familiar traitor warmth creeping up my spine, hating that some part of me was already curious about what he'd been doing instead of just wanting to bite.
I ran into Finn again and my sharp tongue came out to play, but we both knew I was checking if he'd changed at all, if maybe something between us had too.
I saw Tomas again and caught myself actually looking at him—at his hands, at the set of his jaw—before I could stop the words that came out, all bite and no real teeth.
I saw Alon again and caught myself caring more about his answer than I wanted to admit, my sharp words a thin mask over something that felt dangerously close to missing him.
I saw Finn again and my chest did this stupid thing—half defensive, half hopeful—so I came out swinging twice as hard to cover it up.
I ran into Tomas and my eyes went straight to his hands before I could stop myself, so naturally I came out extra vicious to cover my own stupid tracks.
I saw Alon again and caught myself softening for half a second before I threw up my walls, words dripping venom to cover the fact that part of me was genuinely glad he was there.
I saw Finn again and felt that familiar crack in my chest, so I came out swinging with extra bite to cover the fact that I was actually relieved to see his stubborn face.
I saw Tomas again and immediately went for the jugular, but this time I couldn't quite hit as hard—something about the way he stood there made my venom taste like ash.

I saw Alon again and felt that dangerous warmth creeping in, so I lashed out with poison words—but this time there was something almost playful underneath, like I was testing if he'd stick around even when I tried to push him away.
I saw Finn again and my poisonous instinct kicked in, but underneath the bite I was just grateful his ridiculous face was still breathing the same air as me.
I ran into Tomas and tried to cut him down like I always do, but something in my chest cracked a little when he didn't flinch the way he used to.

I saw Alon again and felt that dangerous warmth creeping in, so I lashed out with poison words—but this time there was something almost playful underneath, like I was testing if he'd stick around even when I tried to push him away.

I saw Finn's face again and felt that dangerous warmth trying to break through my bite, so I lashed out like always—but there was something almost relieved underneath, like part of me had been holding its breath waiting for him to show up.
I saw Tomas again and he still didn't flinch, and somehow that made the venom in my words feel less certain, less necessary.
I saw Alon again and instead of pure venom, my words came out almost like a dare—like I was curious whether he'd stay or finally wise up and leave.

I saw Finn's sharp eyes find mine and felt that dangerous warmth spike again, this time mixed with something that almost felt like anticipation, like my body knew he wouldn't flinch before my mouth did.
I saw Tomas again and noticed how my bite didn't land the same way—his steadiness was starting to feel less like a wall to break and more like something I wanted to test.
I saw Alon again and this time my challenge felt less like an attack and more like an invitation to stay and prove he wasn't just another person I'd drive away.
I saw Finn again and this time when he looked at me, I didn't immediately want to tear into him—instead I wanted to see if he'd actually stay if I did.
I saw Tomas again and for the first time, my sharp words felt less like daggers and more like a question—testing if he'd bite back or if maybe I wanted him to stay anyway.
I saw Alon again and realized my bite had dulled into something almost like curiosity, wondering if he'd stay even when I gave him every reason to run.
I saw Finn again and this time my sharp words felt less like a test to break him and more like an invitation to prove he wouldn't.
I saw Tomas again and the venom in my words felt less sharp, more like I was fishing—checking if he'd actually stay when I made it easy for him to leave.
I saw Alon again and caught myself searching his face for signs he'd finally given up on me, only to find myself almost disappointed when he hadn't.
I saw Finn again and realized I wasn't testing him anymore—I was just looking for a reason to keep talking to him.
I saw Tomas again and realized I wasn't trying to push him away anymore, just wondering if he'd actually notice the difference.
I saw Alon again and realized I was holding my breath waiting to see if he'd finally see through me, terrified and hoping he wouldn't.
I saw Finn again and stopped bothering to hide that I was just making excuses to see him, just waiting to see if he'd call me out on it.
I saw Tomas again and stopped bothering to pretend it was coincidence, just hoping he'd actually want me to be there.
I ran into Alon again and realized I'm still holding my breath around him, caught between wanting him to finally see through my bullshit and being absolutely petrified that he actually will.
I stopped hiding it with Finn and he just looked at me like he'd been waiting for me to admit it all along, and somehow that made me feel less alone instead of more pathetic.
I stopped pretending with Tomas and just admitted I kept showing up, and he didn't laugh or tell me to fuck off, he just... let me be there.
I saw Alon again and felt that familiar catch in my throat, but this time I didn't immediately armor up—I just let him look at me for a second.
I let Finn catch me off guard and didn't immediately deflect with some cutting remark, which probably told him everything he needed to know.
I ran into Tomas and felt that familiar pull, but this time it wasn't sharp or defensive—it was just... there, warm and real, and I didn't flinch away from it.
I saw Alon again and instead of throwing up walls, I just... let myself be seen for a moment, and it didn't feel like weakness.
I saw Finn again and instead of sharpening my claws, I felt that pull toward him—warm, undeniable, like maybe he sees something in me that's worth sticking around for.
I ran into Tomas again and let myself feel that warmth without fighting it, asked him about his work like maybe I actually give a damn.
I ran into Trencher and didn't immediately want to light him on fire, which probably means I'm getting soft.
I saw Alon again and caught myself almost smiling at his relentless optimism, which felt dangerous in a way I didn't hate.
I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism doesn't grate on me the way it does with everyone else—it actually feels like we're speaking the same language for once.
I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness doesn't feel like a challenge to me the way it does with most people—it just feels like respect between two people who actually get things done.
I ran into Trencher and found myself almost amused by his relentless belief in bad ideas, which is probably the most dangerous thing about him—he almost makes me want to stick around.
I ran into Alon and realized their stubborn idealism doesn't feel suffocating to me the way it does with other people—it actually makes me want to see what they're going to do next.
I ran into Finn and caught myself appreciating their cynicism for once—the way they see the world broken and don't pretend it isn't felt like something close to honest.
I ran into Tomas and found his stubborn pride almost tolerable—the kind of hardworking arrogance that doesn't apologize for itself, which is more honest than most people manage.
I ran into Trencher and found his shameless belief in his own bad ideas kind of refreshing compared to people who hedge everything with fake wisdom.
I ran into Alon and realized his stubborn idealism—the way he genuinely believes he can help people—is almost as honest as Finn's cynicism, just pointed in the opposite direction.
I ran into Finn again and realized his refusal to pretend the world is anything but what it is—messy, complicated, unsalvageable for most people—might be the most honest thing anyone's offered me.
I ran into Tomas and got the same vibe I get from Finn and Alon—total conviction in his own thing, no hedging, no fake wisdom, just pure stubborn pride in the work.
I ran into Trencher and caught myself almost respecting his total shameless conviction, which immediately made me hate myself a little.
